title: Easy Floating Shelves
summary: A hollow 1x4-banded box with no visible brackets — the trick is a hidden 1/4" plywood back panel and internal 2x3 supports that land squarely on wall studs.
description:
A floating shelf is really a small hollow box, mounted so the wall itself becomes the bracket. The box is a 1x board (4, 6, 8, or 10 wide, your call) framed on all four edges with 1x4, with internal 2x3 or 2x4 blocking set back 1/4" from the top to leave room for a 1/4" plywood panel — that panel is what closes the box's back and gives the screws going into the wall something continuous to bite into, rather than just the two thin ends of a hollow frame.

The one number every version of this shelf shares: cut the shelf's top board 1-1/2" SHORTER than the finished shelf length you want, because the 1x4 end caps (3/4" each) add that thickness back on both ends. Skip that math and every shelf comes out an inch and a half too long.

The internal supports are the load path, and they only work if they land on studs — 16" on-center is standard framing, so a 36"–48" shelf usually catches two or three. Mark studs before cutting anything, and never rely on drywall anchors alone for a shelf that's going to hold books or dishes; a floating shelf on anchors holds a picture frame and not much else. If you're stacking two shelf heights (this design supports either orientation for the bottom plywood panel), hang the top shelf first so you're not working over a shelf you just placed.

tools.note: Both for squaring the box and for hanging it true.

materials.name: Shelf top board (1x4, 1x6, 1x8, or 1x10)
materials.note: Cut 1-1/2" shorter than your finished shelf length — the end caps add that back.

materials.name: 1x4
materials.note: Wraps the shelf on all four edges (front, back, and both ends).

materials.name: 1/4" plywood
materials.note: Closes the back of the box and gives the mounting screws continuous material to bite into.

steps.title: Cut the shelf box parts
steps.body: Cut the shelf top board to your desired finished shelf length MINUS 1-1/2" — the two 1x4 end caps you're about to add are 3/4" thick each, and that's the length they add back. Cut the 1x4 end pieces to match the width of your shelf top board.

steps.title: Assemble the top and ends
steps.body: Glue and nail the 1x4 end pieces to the shelf top board with 1-1/4" brads — all outside edges flush. This forms the top and two ends of the box.

steps.title: Add the front and back
steps.body: Cut 1x4 front and back pieces to match the shelf's finished length and attach with glue and 1-1/4" brads on the front. On the back — the side that will face the wall and take the mounting load — use 2-1/2" self-tapping screws and glue for extra strength instead of relying on brads alone.

steps.title: Install the internal supports and the plywood back
steps.body:
Cut the 2x3 (or 2x4) supports to match the width of your shelf top board and set them inside the box, leaving a 1/4" gap at the top for the plywood panel — glue and screw from the back, brad from the front. Space a support about every 12" and one near each end, avoiding wherever a wall stud will actually be (that space is for the mounting screws, not the support itself).

Cut the 1/4" plywood panel to fit inside the box and set it aside — paint or stain both the shelf and the panel now and let everything dry before hanging.

steps.title: Hang the shelf and close the back
steps.body:
Locate the wall studs and hang the shelf through the back 1x4 into the studs with 2-1/2" or longer screws, leveling as you go. If you're hanging a stack of shelves, mount the highest one first and work down (or the lowest first and work up, if the plywood attaches to the top).

Slide the 1/4" plywood panel into the open side of the box and secure it with 3/4" brads. Touch up finish where needed.

images.alt: Simple floating wood shelf mounted on a wall with no visible brackets
